transcript: [doctor] okay maria martin , date of birth 03/05/1946 has been informed and consents to have this visit recorded . the patient is here for follow-up . hi maria , how you've been doing ? [patient] same i guess , pretty good . [doctor] okay , so you got my letter last time on the results . your sugars are not very well controlled . [patient] yeah , i got them . [doctor] okay so did you increase your glimepiride did i increase it yet i'm still taking the 2 mg. [patient] yeah , so we want you to as a letter said from the last time when your a1c was 9.9 , we wanted you to increase it to 4 mg twice a day so let's start that . [doctor] okay , sure . so you want me to take two of the pills now twice a day yes how much of it do you have well i just filled it okay good . so yeah , you can take two of them twice a day of what you have . the new prescription will be for 4 mg twice a day . [patient] okay . great . [doctor] and are you trying to eat healthier , less sugars , less carbohydrates well not really . well i have n't had any pasta i just do n't really like it so we do n't have any in the house we do n't often have bread and if we do we buy wheat bread okay but even the wheat bread try to cut down on the number of slices you eat per day i just usually have 2 in the morning i do n't care for sandwiches much maybe peanut butter sandwiches once in a while yeah i hardly drink drink any sodas great i drink club soda okay self ret water that is what i prefer so that my water i do n't drink regular water okay that's fine . how about your eye exams ? are they up to date ? [patient] yep , i just got new glasses . [doctor] okay , who do you go see ? i did a liberty road to group up there okay the optimum optometrists right they keep changing doctors each time i go a new document each time i got a really nice one i used to see a year ago was that dr. sanchez yeah okay and you saw him not too long ago yeah i just got my prescription from him will see i have the iodine for my eyes cataracts yet okay so he did that and then i had to wait for a month to get healed up and then he wrote my prescription perfect and then you've got the high blood pressure . [patient] yeah a couple of weeks ago was 116 on the top . [doctor] okay well we will recheck it you are on the combination of medications you are on the metoprolol 50 mg once a day you are on the lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20 with 25 mg once a day and you are on the amlodipine 10 mg once a day . are you taking all of those ? [patient] well yeah . [doctor] perfect and then you've got the high cholesterol and the vascular disease in your legs . you're on atorvastatin . are you tolerating it okay ? feet feel like hell yeah all the time my legs are bugging me i ca n't do much walking okay yeah the feet i think is what we call peripheral neuropathy . [patient] also i have the pain up to my hip . [doctor] okay , you have pain in the hips and upper legs ? [patient] yeah , all of it . [doctor] okay . so those do seem relatively stable , right ? and are you trying to get the walking in ? [patient] yeah . [doctor] okay and you are smoking still ? [patient] yeah , still smoking . [doctor] not ready to quit ? [patient] no , not really . [doctor] it's a bad combination i know i'll think about it okay . [patient] okay . [doctor] now let's do my review of systems . you're not having any headaches or dizziness ? [patient] headaches or dizziness ? no . [doctor] okay . [patient] nothing , feeling totally normal . [doctor] good , good and the vision is better with the cataract surgery , that's been good ? not happy with this lens is kind of funny the other one is good hmmm sometimes they can go in and clean up the eye with laser but we'll see what they say okay okay no shortness of breath or coughing coughing here lately congested what is a look like when it comes up it is relatively clear no fever chills no shortness of breath no okay no chest pains or pressure or tightness no nausea vomiting gi symptoms no peeing okay not good you know i'm old it takes me a little more time slower stream but stable yes but i do pee okay several times per day how many times you have to get up at night not too bad just once in a while okay then i have to pee by morning i do tend to drink my drinks in the evening okay alright lets have a look at you okay physical exam carotids are normal . pulses , no bruits . heart exam rhythm is regular , normal rate . s1 and s2 are i do not hear any murmurs today . okay . have you lean forward for me , take some deep breaths in and out . lungs are clear . i do n't hear any rashes or wheezing today . [patient] the coughing got better this morning . [doctor] you cleared out the lungs ? alright . let me take a look at your feet . [patient] okay . [doctor] extremities are without edema . pulses are palpable on the dorsalis pedis , posterior tibial . she has thickened nails . [patient] yeah . [doctor] yeah . mildly dry skin , no skin breakdown . lem me know if you can feel this . [patient] okay . i closed my eyes . [doctor] yeah er okay mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes okay were done with your exam . [patient] okay . [doctor] okay . so again try to keep the feet moisturized . [patient] yeah . [doctor] you do have a little bit of peripheral peripheral neuropathy . there is a medication we can use if they get really bad but you're already on so many medications . [patient] my girl does have some stuff she uses on her feet , she really likes it , it's like a foot scrub or some type of cream . [doctor] i put it on when i think of it after the shower so i can get my own instead that sounds good so for now your sugars we are going to check it we are going to recheck your blood work today , but the sugars last time we checked , the a1c , your blood sugar average was above nine . i really want you to increase the glimepiride . so the ones you have at home , the 2 mg , take two of them twice a day . [patient] okay . [doctor] okay . watch for low sugars , weak , shaky , sweaty . if that happens , let us know . i do n't expect it to . [patient] okay . [doctor] okay . the new prescription i'm going to write is gon na be for the 4 mg pill , so when you get the new one , just 1 pill twice a day . also continue the metformin okay . blood pressure when i rechecked it , i got 140 over 60s . so the numbers at home are better . so we're gon na continue the combination of medications . we'll check kidney function today . for your cholesterol though , you're on the high dose of atorvastatin or lipitor to protect the arteries , so we really wan na keep you on that . [patient] yeah , no problem . [doctor] i want you to quit smoking , but we've had this bowel before . [patient] yeah . [doctor] hmmm and then we will leave everything else the same . we will get the labs today and we will see you back here in 4 months . sound good ? [patient] okay . [doctor] great have a good day ma'am take care of yourself thank you you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Follow-up for diabetes management, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, peripheral vascular disease.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Maria Martin is a 75-year-old female who presents today for follow-up for diabetes.

The patient's blood glucose levels are poorly controlled and her most recent hemoglobin A1C is 9.9%. She denies increasing glimepiride to 4 mg twice a day as advised but confirms she is compliant with 2 mg twice a day. She mostly denies reducing sugar and carbohydrate intake, although she opts for wheat bread, she does not have pasta at home, and she does not drink much soda and instead drinks club soda or seltzer water. She reports that her diabetic eye exams are up to date and she follows with Dr. Sanchez at Optimum Optometrists. She recently underwent cataract surgery and received a new prescription for her glasses, noting improvement in her vision to one eye and displeasure with the lens in her other eye.

She confirms compliancy with her blood pressure medications of metoprolol 50 mg daily, lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide 20/25 mg daily, and amlodipine 10 mg daily. The patient intermittently completes at home blood pressure checks and reported seeing a systolic BP of 116 a few weeks ago. She is also compliant with her atorvastatin for hyperlipidemia and reports tolerating it well.

The patient is still experiencing bilateral hip, thigh, and foot pain related to peripheral neuropathy, noting her mobility is limited but she is walking as tolerated.

Ms. Martin does continue to smoke cigarettes and denies interest in cessation, although she agreed to begin thinking about it.

She is currently experiencing a productive cough with clear sputum that she attributes to her recent congestion.

The patient is also experiencing a slower urine stream, but it appears stable, and she contributes her urinary symptoms to aging. She also has nocturia on occasion which she contributes to her increased fluid intake in the evenings.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Hypertension.
Hyperlipidemia.
Peripheral vascular disease with neuropathy.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Current tobacco smoker.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Metoprolol 50 mg tablet. Take 1 tablet daily.
Lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide 20/25 mg tablet. Take 1 tablet daily.
Amlodipine 10 mg tablet. Take 1 tablet daily.
Atorvastatin tablet.
Metformin.
Glimepiride 4mg tablet. Take 2 tablets by oral route 2 times per day.

VITALS

Blood pressure 140/60.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Respiratory
No rasping, wheezing.

Cardiovascular
Normal heart rate and regular rhythm. Carotids and pulses are normal. S1 and S2 are crisp. No murmurs.

Gastrointestinal
No nausea or vomiting.

Extremities
Bilateral Diabetic Foot Exam: No edema with palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally. No skin breakdown. Thickened nails.
Sensory Examination: Mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes bilaterally.

RESULTS

Hemoglobin A1C 9.9%.

ASSESSMENT

 Type 2 diabetes mellitus.
 Hypertension.
 Hyperlipidemia.
 Peripheral vascular disease.

PLAN

Type 2 diabetes mellitus
Increase glimepiride to 4 mg tablets two times per day. I counselled patient on the signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia, although I don't think she is at a great risk of hypoglycemia. There will be no change to her metformin dose. I counselled the patient on limiting her carbohydrate intake to support improved glycemia. We will recheck her Hemoglobin A1c and complete a kidney function test today.

Peripheral vascular disease
I counselled patient on improving frequency of moisturizing her feet. Improved glycemia would also benefit her neuropathy. If symptoms worsen, we will consider medication to control symptoms, but not at this time due to the patient has already reached polypharmacy.

Hypertension
Blood pressure appears to be well controlled based on checks completed at home. The patient will continue with amlodipine, metoprolol, and lisinopril/hydrochlorothiazide.

Hyperlipidemia
I will continue with atorvastatin as previously prescribed. If patient were to improve her diet, this would likely be beneficial as well.

INSTRUCTIONS

Finish current prescription of glimepiride 2 mg, 2 tablets 2 times per day. Refill will be 4 mg tablet, 1 tablet 2 times per day. Reduce carbohydrate intake overall to improve glycemia. Call the office with questions or any signs or symptoms of hypoglycemia.
notes:
Subjective:
The patient, Maria Martin, a 76-year-old female, presents for follow-up. She reports feeling "pretty good" and has been experiencing peripheral neuropathy in her feet, which she describes as feeling "like hell" all the time. She also reports pain in her hips and upper legs. She denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, coughing, chest pains, pressure, or tightness. She reports being constipated and having a slow stream of urine, but states it is stable. She has had cataract surgery, which has improved her vision, but is not satisfied with the new lens. She has not had a fever, chills, or nausea.

Past medical history: 
The patient has a history of diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, and vascular disease in her legs. She has been taking glimepiride 2mg twice a day, metoprolol 50mg once a day, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20/25mg once a day, amlodipine 10mg once a day, and atorvastatin. She is a smoker.

Review of systems:
The patient denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, coughing, chest pains, pressure, or tightness. She reports constipation and a slow stream of urine, but states it is stable.

Current medications:
- Glimepiride 2mg twice a day
- Metoprolol 50mg once a day
- Lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide 20/25mg once a day
- Amlodipine 10mg once a day
- Atorvastatin

Objective:
Vital signs:
- Blood pressure: 140/60s (rechecked)
- Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
- Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
- Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam:
- Carotids are normal.
- Pulses are palpable on the dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial.
- Extremities are without edema.
- Mildly dry skin, no skin breakdown.
- Mildly decreased sensation in the tips of the first and second toes.
- Thickened nails.

Test Results:
- [NOT MENTIONED]

Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has poorly controlled diabetes, hypertension, high cholesterol, and vascular disease in her legs. She has peripheral neuropathy in her feet and pain in her hips and upper legs.

Problem list: Diabetes mellitus; hypertension; hypercholesterolemia; peripheral vascular disease; peripheral neuropathy; constipation.

Plan:
I want the patient to increase her glimepiride to 4mg twice a day and to take two of the 2mg pills until she gets the new prescription. I also want her to continue taking metformin. I will recheck her blood pressure and check her kidney function. I will continue her on the high dose of atorvastatin to protect her arteries. I will get the labs today and see her back in 4 months.

Follow-up:
We will see the patient back in 4 months.

She has not increased her glimepiride dose as previously instructed, and her current dose is 2 mg twice a day. She has been trying to eat healthier, but not successfully, as she still consumes wheat bread and occasionally has peanut butter sandwiches. She has not made any significant changes to her diet. She reports being up to date on her eye exams, having recently obtained new glasses and undergoing cataract surgery. Her high blood pressure has been stable, with a recent reading of 116/ unknown. She is currently on a combination of medications, including metoprolol, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. She reports tolerating atorvastatin, but experiencing peripheral neuropathy in her feet and legs, as well as pain in her hips and upper legs. She is a smoker and has not attempted to quit. She denies headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, or chest pains. She reports occasional difficulty urinating and occasional nocturia.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has poorly controlled diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, with evidence of peripheral neuropathy and vascular disease in the legs.

Problem list: Diabetes mellitus type 2; hypertension; hyperlipidemia; peripheral neuropathy; vascular disease in the legs; cataracts.

Plan:
I increased the patient's glimepiride dose to 4 mg twice a day and instructed her to take two of the 2 mg pills until she receives the new prescription. I also instructed her to continue metformin. I advised her to watch for signs of low blood sugar, including weakness, shakiness, and sweating. I wrote a new prescription for 4 mg glimepiride once a day, replacing the previous 2 mg dose. I continued the patient on her current medications, including metoprolol, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide, and amlodipine. I also instructed her to continue atorvastatin. I will recheck her blood work, including kidney function, and see her back in 4 months.

Follow-up:
The patient will be seen in 4 months to recheck her blood work and medications.

She confirmed that she has been taking her prescribed glimepiride, 2 mg twice a day, but we want to increase it to 4 mg twice a day. She also reported that she has not been making significant changes to her diet, eating fewer sugars and carbohydrates. She mentioned that she has had cataract surgery and is seeing an optometrist at Liberty Road to Group, where she sees a different doctor each time. She has high blood pressure, which she monitors at home and reported a reading of 116/ top. She is on multiple medications, including metoprolol, lisinopril with hydrochlorothiazide, amlodipine, and atorvastatin. She has peripheral neuropathy in her feet, which causes pain in her legs and feet, and has difficulty walking. She is a smoker and has not shown a desire to quit. She denied headaches, dizziness, shortness of breath, coughing, fever, chills, chest pain, nausea, vomiting, and GI symptoms. She reported difficulty peeing, but stated that it is stable and not a significant concern. She denied any recent coughing or wheezing.
